tests.name: The use of Coordinates - 01
tests.instructionHtml:
• Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The distance of the point P(-3, 4) from x-axis is
questions.explanation:
The distance of the point P(-3,4) from the x-axis
= Y- coordinate of the point 
= 4 units

questions.question: The point on the x-axis which is equidistant from points (-1, 0) and (5, 0) is
questions.explanation:
Let the required point be P(x, 0). Then,
{tex}P A^{2}=P B^{2} \Rightarrow(x+1)^{2}=(x-5)^{2}\\ \Rightarrow x^{2}+2 x+1=x^{2}-10 x+25\\ \Rightarrow 12 x=24 \Rightarrow x=2{/tex}
So, the required point is P(2, 0).

questions.question: The point (-2, 5) lies in
questions.explanation: For any point to lie in the second quadrant, Abscissa and ordinate must be (-, +). So the given point (-2, 5) lies in 2nd quadrant.

questions.question: The point of intersect of the coordinate axes is
questions.explanation: The point where coordinate axes intersect is known as origin O(0,0).

questions.question: Which of the points A(-5, 0), B(0, -3), C(3, 0), D(0, 4) are closer to the origin?
questions.explanation:
Points B and C both
As per distance formulla, the distance between the points P(a, b) and Q(x, y) is given by:
|PQ| = {tex}\sqrt {(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}{/tex}
If O(0. 0) be the origin, then we have
|AO| = {tex}\sqrt {(-5)^2}=5{/tex}
|BO| = {tex}\sqrt {(-3)^2}=3{/tex}
|CO| = {tex}\sqrt {3^2}=3{/tex}
|DO| = {tex}\sqrt {(4)^2}=4{/tex}
This shows that B and C are both at minimum distance from the origin.

questions.question: For a point {tex}(3,-5){/tex}, the value of (abscissa - ordinate) is:
If x is a positive integer such that the distance between points P (x, 2) and Q (3, - 6) is 10 units, then x =
questions.explanation:
Distance between P(x, 2) and Q(3, -6) = 10 units
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(x_{2}-{x}_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(3-x)^{2}+(-6-2)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(3-x)^{2}+(-8)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(3-x)^{2}+64}=10{/tex}
Squaring both sides,
(3 - x)2 + 64 = 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 + x2 - 6x + 64 - 100 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 6x - 27 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 9x + 3x - 27 = 0 {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} \because-27 &=-9 \times 3 \\ -6 &=-9+3 \end{aligned}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x - 9) + 3(x - 9) = 0
(x - 9) (x + 3) = 0
Either x - 9 = 0, then x = 9 or x + 3 = 0, then x = -3
x is positive integer
Hence x = 9

questions.question: Ordinate of a point is negative in
questions.explanation:
Since, sign of point in 3rd quadrant is (-, -).
And in 4th quadrant, it is (+, -).
So, Ordinate of a point is -ve only in 3rd and 4th quadrant.

questions.question: If the point (x, 4) lies on a circle whose centre is at the origin and radius is 5 then x =
questions.explanation:
Point A(x, 4) is on a circle with centre O(0, 0) and radius = 5
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{OA}=\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(4-0)}=\sqrt{x^{2}+16}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sqrt{x^{2}+16}=5 \Rightarrow x^{2}+16=25{/tex}
Squaring on both sides, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=25-16=9=(\pm 3)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore~ x=\pm 3{/tex}

questions.question: The distance of the point P (4, 3) from the origin is
questions.explanation:
Using Pythagorous theorem: OP2 = OQ2 + QP2
OP2 = 42 + 32
{tex}\mathrm{OP}^{2}=\sqrt{16+9}=5{/tex}

questions.question: If A and B are the points (-6, 7) and (-1, -5) respectively, then the distance 2AB is equal to
questions.explanation:
2AB = {tex} 2\sqrt {{{\left( { - 1 + 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 5 - 7} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}2\sqrt {25 + 144} {/tex}
= {tex}2\sqrt {169} {/tex}
= 26 units

questions.question: The linear equation x = 3y + 5 cuts the x-axis at ________.
questions.explanation: : The equation x = 3y + 5 cuts the x-axis at (x, 0). {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5

questions.question: The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant III are
questions.explanation:
The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant III are both —ve, 

i.e, in quadrant III co-ordinates Of point is (—,—).

questions.question: If A is a point on the x-axis whose abscissa is 5 and B is the point (1, -3), then the distance AB is
questions.explanation:
A is a point on the x-axis, therefore coordinates of A are (5, 0)
Here, A (5, 0) and B{tex}\left( {1, - 3} \right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AB = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {1 - 5} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3 - 0} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {16 + 9} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {25} {/tex} = 5 units

questions.question: Which of the following are the signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant I?
questions.explanation: The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant I are both +ve i.e. (+, +)

questions.question: The distance of the point P(-6, 8) from origin is:
questions.explanation:
The distance of the point P( -6,8) from the origin (0, 0)
{tex}=\sqrt{(-6)^{2}+8^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{36+64}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{100}{/tex}
= 10

questions.question: The perimeter of the triangle formed by the vertices {tex}(0,0),(2,0){/tex} and {tex}(0,2){/tex} is:
questions.explanation: {tex}(4+2 \sqrt{2}){/tex} units

questions.question: The points P(0, 6), Q(-5, 3) and R( 3,1) are the vertices of a triangle, which is
questions.explanation:
{tex}PQ=\sqrt{(-5)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}=\sqrt{2 5+3}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{34}{/tex}
{tex}QR=\sqrt{R^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{64+4}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{6 8}{/tex}
{tex}PR=\sqrt{3^{2}+5^{2}}=\sqrt{9+25}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{34}{/tex}
PQ = PR
QR2 = PQ2 + PR2
{tex}(\sqrt{68})^{2}=(\sqrt{34})^{2}+(\sqrt{34})^{2}{/tex}
68 = 68
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is a Isosceles triangle.

questions.question: The distance of the point (5, 12) from the y-axis is
questions.explanation: The distance of any point from {tex}y - {/tex}axis is its abscissa. Therefore, the required distance is 5 units.

questions.question: The abscissa of any point on y-axis is
questions.explanation: The abscissa of any point on y-axis is always zero. This means that this point hasn't covered any distance on x-axis.

questions.question: The point C(-5, -2) lies in
questions.explanation:
In 3rd quadrant values of Abscissa and Ordinate both are —ve , i.e, (— , +)

Here,since value of both x and y-coordinate are -ve so it will lies in 3rd quadrant

questions.question: Point (0, –7) lies
questions.explanation: In point (0, -7) x - coordinate is zero, so it lies on Y-axis and y-coordinate is negative, so the point (0, – 7) lies on the Y-axis in the negative direction.

questions.question: AOBC is a rectangle whose three vertices are {tex}{A}(0,2), {O}(0,0){/tex} and {tex}{B}(4,0){/tex}. The square of the length of its diagonal is equal to:
(2, 1) is a point, which belongs to the line ________.
questions.explanation: (2, 1) is a point, which satisfies the eq. of line 2y = x. So, it belongs to the line 2y = x.

questions.question: The value of x in the ordered pair (x, -8) if the ordinate of the pair is 4 more than the abscissa is ________.
questions.explanation: According to question, we have x = -8 - 4 = -12

questions.question: The area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} AOB having vertices A(0, 6), 0(0, 0) and B(6, 0) is
questions.explanation:
18 sq units
When we plot the given points in the graph paper then,
 is the right angle triangle, where
OB = Base = 6 units
Height of triangle = OA = 6 units
∴ Area of  {tex}\triangle AOB = \frac12 \times OA \times OB{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Area of {tex} \triangle AOB = \frac12 \times 6 \times 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Area of {tex} \triangle AOB=\frac12 \times 36{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle AOB = 18{/tex} square units

questions.question: The distance between the points (cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) and (sin{tex}\theta{/tex}, - cos{tex}\theta{/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
Distance between {tex}(\cos \theta, \sin \theta){/tex} and {tex}(\sin \theta,-\cos \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(-\cos \theta-\sin \theta)^{2}+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\left\{\because \sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1\right\}{/tex}

questions.question: The distance of point {tex}(-3,4){/tex} from {tex}y{/tex}-axis is:
If the point P(x, y) is equidistant from A(5, 1) and B(-1, 5), then
questions.explanation:
Let the point C{tex}\left( {x,y} \right){/tex} is equidistant from the points A (5, 1) and B{tex}\left( { - 1,5} \right).{/tex}

i.e. AC = BC
{tex}\therefore AC^2 = BC^2{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}{\left( {5 - x} \right)^2} + {\left( {1 - y} \right)^2} = {\left( { - 1 - x} \right)^2} + {\left( {5 - y} \right)^2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}25 + {x^2} - 10x + 1 + {y^2}- 2y = 1 + {x^2} + 2x + 25 + {y^2} - 10y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex} - 10x - 2y - 2x + 10y = 26 - 26{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex} - 12x + 8y = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex} - 12x = - 8y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}3x = 2y{/tex}

questions.question: The equation of x-axis is
questions.explanation:
In co-ordinate, any point P is written as p(x, y).
When Y = 0 then the point P lies on x-axis.
So, Y = 0 is the equation of the x-axis.

questions.question: The point P(3, 0) lies
questions.explanation:
In Cartesian plane, any point p is written as p(x, y).
If the value of y-coordinate (ordinate) is zero then the point will lie on the x-axis.
So here point p(3, 0) will lie on positive x-axis

questions.question: P(5, -7) be a point on the graph. Draw the PM {tex}\perp{/tex} y-axis. The coordinates of M are
questions.explanation:
Here, PM Perpendicular to y-axis.
So point M lies on the y-axis, and for any point on y-axis always the value of x = 0.
So Co-ordinate of M = (0, -7).

questions.question: If A(2, 3) and B(-3, 4), then (abscissa of A) – (abscissa of B) is
questions.explanation:
Here we have, the abscissa of A = 2 and abscissa of B = -3.
So, according to question,
(abscissa of A) - (abscissa of B)
= 2 - (-3)
= 5

questions.question: The distance between the points A(2, -3) and B(2, 2) is
questions.explanation:
Distance between
A(2, -3) and B(2, 2) is
AB = {tex}\sqrt{(2-2)^2+(2-(-3))^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{0+(2+3)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex}
= 5 units.

questions.question: In the fourth quadrant,
questions.explanation:
In the fourth quadrant, x is positive, y is negative.
i.e the value of x is called abscissa which is positive and the value of y is called coordinate which is negative in the 4th quadrant

questions.question: The distance between the points {tex}(0,2 \sqrt{5}){/tex} and {tex}(-2 \sqrt{5}, 0){/tex} is
questions.explanation: {tex}2 \sqrt{10}{/tex} units

questions.question: Point(1, 1), (1,-1), (-1, 1), (-1,-1)

options.html: do not lie in the same quadrant
lie in I and III quadrants

questions.explanation: do not lie in the same quadrant

questions.question: The point of intersection of X and Y axes is called
questions.explanation:
In two dimensions, On the flat coordinate plane, there are two axes,
the vertical y-axis and the horizontal x-axis.
The origin is the point where they intersect.
This point has the coordinates (0,0) and is usually labelled with the letter O.

questions.question: The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant IV are
questions.explanation:
In the Cartesian plane, if co-ordinate of any point P is P(x, y), then x-coordinate is called the abscissa of the point and, y-coordinate is called ordinate of the point.
The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant IV is (+, -)

questions.question: Points (6, 8), (3, 7), (-2, -2) and (1, -1) are joined to form a quadrilateral. What will be the structure of the quadrilateral?
questions.explanation:
Let the points be A(6, 8), B(3, 7), C(-2, -2) and D(1, -1).

Now, AB = {tex}\sqrt{(3-6)^2+(7-8)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {10}{/tex}
BC = {tex}\sqrt{(-2-3)^2+(-2-7)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {106}{/tex}
CD = {tex}\sqrt{(1+2)^2+(-1+2)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {10}{/tex}
DA = {tex}\sqrt{(6-1)^2+(8+1)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {106}{/tex}
Also, AC = {tex}\sqrt{(-8)^2+(-10)^2}{/tex}  = {tex}\sqrt{64+100}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{164}{/tex}
BD = {tex}\sqrt{(-2)^2+(-8)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+64}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{68}{/tex}
Since, AB = DC and BC = DA and AC {tex}\neq{/tex} BD.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ABCD is a parallelogram.

questions.question: Abscissa of all the points on the y-axis is:
questions.explanation:
On y-axis, x-coordinate i.e. abscissa is 0.
Thus, abscissa of all the points on y-axis is 0.

questions.question: The distance between the points (a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, -a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) and (a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
{tex} A(a \cos \theta,-a \sin \theta) {/tex}
{tex} B(a \sin \theta, a \cos \theta) {/tex}
{tex} A B=\sqrt{(a \cos \theta-a \sin \theta)^2+(-a \sin \theta-a \cos \theta)^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{a^2 \cos ^2 \theta+a^2 \sin ^2 \theta-2 a^2 \cos \theta \sin \theta+a^2 \sin ^2 \theta+a^2 \cos ^2 \theta+2 a^2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{2 a^2\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{2 a^2} {/tex}
{tex} A B=a \sqrt{2} \text { units }{/tex}

questions.question: The point which lies on y-axis at a distance of 6 units in the positive direction of y-axis is
questions.explanation:
Since it lies on the y-axis so it's abscissa x will be zero.
Thus, the point will be (0, 6).

questions.question: A circle drawn with origin as the centre passes through ({tex}\frac{13}{2}{/tex}, 0). The point which does not lie in the interior of the circle is
questions.explanation:
Distance between (0, 0) and {tex}\left(-6, \frac{5}{2}\right){/tex} 
d = {tex}\sqrt{(-6-0)^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{2}-0\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{36+\frac{25}{4}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{144+25}{4}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{169}{4}}=\frac{13}{2}{/tex} = 6.5
So, the point {tex}\left(-6, \frac{5}{2}\right){/tex} does not lie in the circle.

questions.question: The distance between the points (-1, -5) and (-6, 7) is
questions.explanation:
Let point A be (x1, y1) = (-1, -5) and point B (x2, y2) = (-6, 7)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Distance between A and B  
Using Distance Formula
= {tex}\sqrt {(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}{/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {(-6+1)^2 + (7+5)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {25 + 144}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {169} {/tex} = 13 units

questions.explanation:

Since, x > 0 and y > 0 
So, both x and y co-ordinate are +ve,
And we know that in 1st quadrant value of both Abscissa and Ordinate is +ve so, the point will lie in 1st quadrant.

questions.explanation:

But point ( -2, -5) lies in 3rd quadrant since,sign of abscissa and ordinate in 3rd quadrant is ( -, -).

questions.question: The distance between the points (a, b) and (-a, -b) is
questions.explanation:
The distance between the points (a, b) and (-a, -b)
= {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( { - a - a} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - b - b} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {4{a^2} + 4{b^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}2\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} {/tex}

questions.explanation: I quadrant asx>0,  y <0 then (x, -y) lies in Ist quadrant

questions.question: ABC is a triangle. B lies on x -axis at a distance of 4 units from y -axis at its right. {tex}C{/tex} is on {tex}y{/tex}-axis and it is 3 units away from the origin. If the coordinates of A are (0,0), the perimeter of {tex}\triangle {ABC}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation: 12 units

questions.question: The distance of the point (-3, 4) from the origin is
questions.explanation:
Let the given point be (x1, y1) = (-3, 4) and the origin is (x2, y2) = (0, 0)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} The distance of the given point from the origin = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {0 + 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {0 - 4} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {9 + 16}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {25} {/tex} = 5 units

questions.question: If the distance between the points (p, -5) and (2, 7) is 13 units, then the value of p is
questions.explanation:
Let point A be (p, -5) and point B (2, 7) and distance between A and B = 13 units
{tex}\therefore {/tex} 13 = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {2 - p} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {7 + 5} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 13 = {tex}\sqrt {4 + {p^2} - 4p + 144} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 13 = {tex}\sqrt {{p^2} - 4p + 148} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 169 = p2 - 4p + 48
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} p2 - 4p - 21 = 0
= p2 - 7p + 3p - 21= 0
= p(p - 7) + 3(p - 7) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (p - 7)(p + 3) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} p = 7, p = -3

for x = 4, we have
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 + 4
= 12 + 4
= 16 {tex}\neq{/tex} 12
Hence (4, 12) doesn't lie on the given line.

{tex}-20 x-60=x=3{/tex}

questions.question: The points (-4, 0), (4, 0) and (0, 3) are the vertices of a:
questions.explanation:
AB2 = (4 + 4)2 + (0 - 0)2 = 82 + 02 = 64 + 0 = 64
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB={tex}\sqrt{64}{/tex}= 8 units
BC2 = (0-4)2 + (3-0)2 = (-4)2 + 32 = 16 + 9 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 units.
AC2 = (0 + 4)2 + (3 - 0)2 = 42 + 32 = 16 + 9 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A C = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 units.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is isosceles.

questions.question: If the coordinates of a point are (3, -7), then its ordinate is
questions.explanation:

questions.explanation: Coordinate Geometry is an algebraic tool for studying geometry.

questions.question: If end points of a diameter of a circle are (-5, 4) and (1, 0), then the radius of the circle is:
questions.explanation:
Distance between two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is {tex}\sqrt {(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}{/tex}
Diameter = {tex}\sqrt {(1 - (-5))^2 + (0 - 4)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {36 + 16}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {52}{/tex} = {tex}2\sqrt {13}{/tex}
radius = {tex}\frac {\text {diameter}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {2\sqrt {13}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {13}{/tex}

questions.explanation: {tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordinate of A(0, -4)

questions.question: The distance of the point point (3, 4) from the origin is
questions.explanation:
The distance of the point (3, 4) from origin is
{tex} =\sqrt{3^2+4^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{9+16}=\sqrt{25}{/tex}
= 5 unit

questions.question: The distance between the points A (0, 6) and B (0, –2) is
questions.explanation:
By using the distance formula:

d2 = (x2 - x1)2 + (y2 - y1)2
Lets calculate the distance between the points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2)
We have;
x1 = 0, x2 = 0
y1 = 6, y2 = - 2
d2 = (0 – 0)2 + ( - 2 - 6)2 
d = {tex}\sqrt{(0)^{2}+(-8)^{2}}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\sqrt{64}{/tex}
d = 8 units
So, the distance between A (0, 6) and B (0, - 2) = 8

questions.question: If (x + 7, 3) = (5, 6 + y), then the values of x and y respectively are
questions.explanation:
Since (x + 7, 3) = (5, 6 + y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 7 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5 - 7 = -2
and 6 + y = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 - 6 = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = -2, y = -3

questions.question: If the distance between the points A(4, p) and B(1, 0) is 5 units, then the value(s) of p is (are)
questions.explanation:
Distance between (4, p) and (1, 0) = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}=5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(1-4)^{2}+(0-p)^{2}}=5{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+(-p)^{2}}=5{/tex}
Squaring, both sides
(-3)2 + (-p)2 = (5)2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 + p2 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 = 25 - 9 = 16
{tex}\therefore p=\pm \sqrt{16}=\pm 4{/tex}

questions.question: The distance between the points A(5, -4) and B(4, -5) is
questions.explanation:
A (5, -4) B (4, -5)
AB = {tex} \sqrt{(5-4)^2+(-4+5)^2}{/tex}
= {tex} \sqrt{1^2+1^2}{/tex}
AB = {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} units

questions.question: Which type of slope indicates a linear decay?
questions.explanation: A linear decay is represented by a straight line with a negative slope.

questions.question: How many distinct points are minimally required to plot a straight line on a coordinate plane?
questions.explanation: To plot a linear equation as a straight line, it is minimally required to identify any two distinct points on the line.

questions.question: A polynomial containing three non-zero terms is called a ________.
questions.explanation:
A polynomial containing three non-zero terms is called a trinomial.
Example: 5x2 + 2x + 3, ax2 = bx + c, 3x + 2y - 3

questions.question: A symbol having a fixed value is called a ______.
questions.explanation:
A symbol of having a fixed value is called a constant. Ex. Any natural number, whole number, integers, rational number.
A symbol having the variable values is called variable.

questions.question: Which of the following is a polynomial.
questions.explanation: -4 is a constant polynomial of degree zero.

questions.question: Degree of the polynomial 2x4 + 3x3 - 5x2 + 9x + 1 is
questions.explanation: The highest power of the variable is 4. So, the degree of the polynomial is 4.

questions.question: What is the perimeter of a square with side length 'y'?
questions.explanation:
The perimeter of a square is the sum of its four equal sides.
If the side length is 'y', the perimeter is y + y + y + y = 4y.

questions.question: A linear polynomial is given by P(t) = 3t + 8. If the value of 't' changes from 2 to 4, how does the value of P(t) change?
questions.explanation:
When t = 2, P(2) = 3(2) + 8 = 6 + 8 = 14.
When t = 4, P(4) = 3(4) + 8 = 12 + 8 = 20.
The change in the value of the polynomial is: 20 - 14 = 6.
Therefore, P(t) increases by 6.

questions.question: A production line has a fixed setup cost and a variable cost per item produced. If 100 items cost ₹800 to produce and 250 items cost ₹1700 to produce, what is the fixed setup cost?
questions.explanation:
Let 'y' be the total cost and 'x' be the number of items produced.
The relationship is {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}, where 'b' is the fixed setup cost and 'a' is the variable cost per item.
For 100 items: {tex}800 = 100a + b{/tex} (Equation 1)
For 250 items: {tex}1700 = 250a + b{/tex}
(Equation 2) Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2: {tex}(1700 - 800) = (250a - 100a) + (b - b){/tex}
{tex}900 = 150a{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{900}{150}{/tex}
{tex}a = 6{/tex}
The variable cost per item 'a' is ₹6.
Substitute {tex}a = 6{/tex} into Equation 1: {tex}800 = 100(6) + b{/tex}
{tex}800 = 600 + b{/tex}
{tex}b = 800 - 600{/tex}
{tex}b = 200{/tex}
The fixed setup cost 'b' is ₹200.

questions.question: A rectangular plot has a fixed breadth of 12 m. If its length is 'l' meters, what is the linear pattern representing the perimeter of the plot?
questions.explanation:
Given:
Breadth = 12 m
Length = 'l' meters
The perimeter of a rectangle is {tex}2 \times (\text{length} + \text{breadth}){/tex}.
Substituting the values:
The perimeter is {tex}2(l + 12){/tex}
Therefore, the linear expression representing the perimeter is: {tex}2(l + 12){/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following is a polynomial ?
questions.explanation: 0 is a polynomial whose degree is not defined.

questions.question: Zero of the zero polynomial is
questions.explanation:
Zero of the zero polynomial is any real number.
e.g., Let us consider zero polynomial x - k, where k is a real number. For determining the zero, put x - k = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = k Hence, zero of the zero polynomial be any real number.

questions.question: The relationship between two units of measurement, 'A' and 'B', can be expressed as a linear equation A = aB + b. If 'A' is 50 when 'B' is 10, and 'A' is 90 when 'B' is 20, find the values of 'a' and 'b'.
questions.explanation:
Given the linear relationship {tex}A = aB + b{/tex}.
When {tex}B = 10, A = 50{/tex}. So, {tex}50 = 10a + b{/tex} (Equation 1)
When {tex}B = 20, A = 90{/tex}. So, {tex}90 = 20a + b{/tex} (Equation 2)
Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2: {tex}(90 - 50) = (20a - 10a) + (b - b){/tex}
{tex}40 = 10a{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{40}{10}{/tex}
{tex}a = 4{/tex}
Substitute {tex}a = 4{/tex} into Equation 1: {tex}50 = 10(4) + b{/tex}
{tex}50 = 40 + b{/tex}
{tex}b = 50 - 40{/tex}
{tex}b = 10{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}a = 4{/tex} and {tex}b = 10{/tex}.

questions.question: A submarine starts at a depth of 50 meters and descends at 15 meters per minute. The depth {tex}D(t){/tex} after {tex}t{/tex} minutes is given by {tex}D(t) = 50 + 15t{/tex}. At what depth will the submarine be after 8 minutes?
questions.explanation:
Given the depth function {tex}D(t) = 50 + 15t{/tex}.
To find the depth after 8 minutes, substitute {tex}t = 8{/tex} into the function.
{tex}D(8) = 50 + 15(8){/tex}
{tex}D(8) = 50 + 120{/tex}
{tex}D(8) = 170{/tex}
So, the submarine will be at a depth of 170 meters after 8 minutes.

questions.question: The equation {tex}y = ax + b{/tex} represents a linear relationship. What does the variable {tex}a{/tex} typically represent in this equation?

options.html: A constant term unrelated to the line's orientation

questions.explanation: In the general form of a linear equation {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}, the coefficient {tex}a{/tex} represents the slope of the line.

questions.question: A baker starts with 100 kg of flour. Each day, they use 8 kg of flour. How many days will it take for the baker to have 20 kg of flour remaining?
questions.explanation:
Let 'd' be the number of days.
The initial amount of flour is 100 kg.
The amount used each day is 8 kg.
So, the amount remaining after 'd' days is {tex}100 - 8d{/tex}
The baker will have 20 kg of flour remaining when: {tex}100 - 8d = 20{/tex}
{tex}100 - 20 = 8d{/tex}
{tex}80 = 8d{/tex}
{tex}d = 80 / 8{/tex}
{tex}d = 10{/tex}
It will take 10 days.

questions.question: A journey costs according to the function {tex}C(d) = 80 + 35d{/tex}, where {tex}C{/tex} is the cost in rupees and {tex}d{/tex} is the distance in km. If someone travels 20 km, what will be the total cost of the journey?
questions.explanation:
Given the cost function {tex}C(d) = 80 + 35d{/tex}.
To find the cost for traveling 20 km, substitute {tex}d = 20{/tex} into the function.
{tex}C(20) = 80 + 35(20){/tex}
{tex}C(20) = 80 + 700{/tex}
{tex}C(20) = 780{/tex}
So, the total cost for traveling 20 km is ₹780.

questions.question: A car rental company charges a daily base fee of ₹40 plus ₹0.15 per kilometer driven. Construct a linear expression representing the total cost for driving 'k' kilometers in one day.
questions.explanation: The base fee is ₹40. The cost per kilometer is ₹0.15. So for 'k' kilometers, the cost is ₹0.15k. The total cost is the sum of the base fee and the per-kilometer cost, which is ₹0.15k + 40.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a linear pattern where a quantity decreases by a constant amount over equal intervals?
questions.explanation: Linear decay describes a linear pattern where a quantity decreases by a constant amount over equal intervals.

questions.question: The initial temperature of a chemical solution is {tex}15^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. It is cooled at a steady rate of {tex}2^{\circ} {C}{/tex} per minute. Which linear function {tex}T(x){/tex} represents the temperature of the solution after {tex}x{/tex} minutes?
questions.explanation:
{tex}T(x)=\text{Initial Temperature}+(\text{Rate of Change}\times x){/tex}

Substituting our known values:
{tex}T(x)=15+(-2\times x){/tex}
{tex}T(x)=15-2x{/tex}

questions.question: The degree of the polynomial {tex}4{x^4} + 0{x^3} + 0{x^2} + 5x + 7{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
4x4 + 0x3 + 0x2 + 5x + 7
=  4x4 + 5x + 7
Here, the height power is 4.
Therefore, the degree of given polynomial is 4.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a polynomial?
questions.explanation: Cearly, {tex}x+\frac{3}{x}{/tex} is not a polynomial.

questions.question: The degree of constant function is
questions.explanation:
The degree of any constant term 5 (say)

We can write 5 as 5 x 1 = 5xo          [Since ao= 1]

Therefore the degree of any constant term is 0

questions.question: Which one of the following is a polynomial?
questions.explanation:
Since the power of the variable of all terms of a polynomial should be a whole number. Then
{tex}x^2+\frac{3 x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex}
= {tex}x^2+3 x^{\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}x^2+3 x^{\frac{2}{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}{x^2} + 3x{/tex}
Here the powers of variable are whole numbers. Therefore the given expression is a polynomial.

questions.question: The degree of a constant polynomial is
questions.explanation: The degree of a constant polynomial is 0. e.g. 5 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex}1 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} xo

questions.question: A scientist is studying the growth of a certain plant. The height of the plant 'h' (in cm) after 'd' days follows a linear relationship {tex}h = ad + b{/tex}. On day 5, the plant was 18 cm tall. On day 12, it was 39 cm tall. What was the initial height of the plant (on day 0)?
questions.explanation:
Let 'h' be the height and 'd' be the number of days.
The linear relationship is {tex}h = ad + b{/tex}.
On day 5: When {tex}d = 5, h = 18{/tex}. So, {tex}18 = 5a + b{/tex} (Equation 1)
On day 12: When {tex}d = 12, h = 39{/tex}. So, {tex}39 = 12a + b{/tex} (Equation 2)
Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2: {tex}(39 - 18) = (12a - 5a) + (b - b){/tex}
{tex}21 = 7a{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{21}{7}{/tex}
{tex}a = 3{/tex}
The growth rate 'a' is 3 cm/day.
Substitute {tex}a = 3{/tex} into Equation 1: {tex}18 = 5(3) + b{/tex}
{tex}18 = 15 + b{/tex}
{tex}b = 18 - 15{/tex}
{tex}b = 3{/tex}
The initial height 'b' (on day 0, when d=0) was 3 cm.

questions.question: Coefficient of x2 in (x2 -1) (x - 2) is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}(x^2-1)(x-2)=x^3-2x^2-x+2{/tex}

Therefore, coefficient of {tex}x^2=-2{/tex}

questions.question: Consider the polynomial 4x - 7. If this is treated as an input-output process, what is the 'output' when the 'input' (x) is 5?
questions.explanation: To find the output, substitute x = 5 into the polynomial: 4(5) - 7 = 20 - 7 = 13.

questions.question: A company's revenue {tex}R(q){/tex} from selling {tex}q{/tex} units of a product is given by {tex}R(q) = 25q - C{/tex}, where {tex}C{/tex} is the initial fixed cost. If the initial fixed cost is ₹150 and the revenue is ₹500, how many units were sold?
questions.explanation:
Given the revenue function {tex}R(q) = 25q - C{/tex}.
The initial fixed cost {tex}C = 150{/tex}.
So, the function is {tex}R(q) = 25q - 150{/tex}.
We are given that the revenue is ₹500, so {tex}R(q) = 500{/tex}.
{tex}500 = 25q - 150{/tex}
Add 150 to both sides: {tex}500 + 150 = 25q{/tex}
{tex}650 = 25q{/tex}
Divide by 25: {tex}q = \frac{650}{25}{/tex}
{tex}q = 26{/tex}
Therefore, 26 units were sold.

questions.question: What is the value of {tex}P(x)=2 x+1{/tex} when {tex}x=3{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Substitute {tex}x=3{/tex} :

{tex} P(3)=2(3)+1=6+1=7{/tex}

Therefore, the value is 7.

questions.question: The degree of the polynomial 5x3 - 3x2 - x + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: The degree of the polynomial 5x3 - 3x2 - x + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} is 3. The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of that polynomial.

questions.question: The degree of the polynomial (x3 - 2)(x2 - 11) is
questions.explanation:
(x3 - 2)(x2 - 11)
= x3(x2 - 11) - 2(x2 - 11)
= x5 - 11x3 - 2x2 + 22
Here the highest power is 5.
Therefore, the degree is 5.

questions.question: 8 is a polynomial of degree
questions.explanation:
Since 8 is a constant term.
Therefore its degree is 0.

questions.question: Two numbers sum up to 85. One of the numbers is 15 more than the other. What are the two numbers?
questions.explanation:
Let the smaller number be {tex}x{/tex}.

Then the larger number is:

{tex} x+15{/tex}

Given:

{tex}x+(x+15)=85{/tex}
{tex}2 x+15=85{/tex}
{tex}2 x=70{/tex}
{tex}x=35{/tex}

Larger number:

{tex} 35+15=50{/tex}

questions.question: A painter charges a fixed fee of ₹40 per job plus ₹15 per hour. If {tex}y{/tex} is the total cost and {tex}x{/tex} is the number of hours, what is the linear equation representing this relationship?
questions.explanation: The fixed fee (₹40) represents the y-intercept ({tex}b{/tex}), and the hourly charge (₹15) represents the slope ({tex}a{/tex}). Thus, the equation is {tex}y = 15x + 40{/tex}.

questions.question: An auto-rickshaw fare starts at ₹30 and remains the same for the initial 2 km. Then it increases by ₹8 per km. What will be the total fare for a travel of 9 km?
questions.explanation:
The initial fare for 2 km is ₹30.
For a travel of 9 km, the additional distance is 9 - 2 = 7 km.
The fare for the additional distance is 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = ₹56.
The total fare is ₹30 + ₹56 = ₹86.

questions.question: Degree of a zero polynomial is:
questions.explanation: not defined

questions.question: The degree of a biquadratic polynomial is
questions.explanation: The biquadratic polynomial is a polynomial of the fourth degree. Biquadratic polynomial = a(x2)2 + b(x)2 + c = ax4 + bx2 + c.

questions.question: A balloon starts at a height of 1500 meters and descends at a constant rate of 120 meters per minute. Formulate a linear function {tex}H(m){/tex} that represents the height of the balloon after {tex}m{/tex} minutes.
questions.explanation: The initial height is 1500 meters. The balloon descends, so the height decreases. The rate of descent is 120 meters per minute. Thus, the function is initial height minus (rate times minutes).

questions.question: What is the general form of a linear polynomial in one variable?
questions.explanation: A linear polynomial in one variable is generally expressed in the form ax + b, where 'a' and 'b' are constants and 'x' is the variable, with 'a' not equal to 0.

questions.question: A rectangle has a fixed length of 15 cm. If its breadth is 'b' cm, what is the linear pattern representing the area of the rectangle?
questions.explanation:
The area of a rectangle is Length × Breadth.
Given the length is 15 cm and the breadth is 'b' cm, the area is {tex}15 \times b = 15b{/tex}.

questions.question: Degrees Celsius ({tex}C{/tex}) and Kelvin ({tex}K{/tex}) are related by a linear equation {tex}K = aC + b{/tex}. Given that 0°C is 273.15 K and 100°C is 373.15 K, find the values of 'a' and 'b'.
questions.explanation:
Given the linear relationship {tex}K = aC + b{/tex}.
When {tex}C = 0, K = 273.15{/tex}.
So, {tex}273.15 = a(0) + b \Rightarrow b = 273.15{/tex} (Equation 1)
When {tex}C = 100, K = 373.15{/tex}.
So, {tex}373.15 = a(100) + b{/tex} (Equation 2)
Substitute {tex}b = 273.15{/tex} from Equation 1 into Equation 2:
{tex}373.15 = 100a + 273.15{/tex}
{tex}100a = 373.15 - 273.15{/tex}
{tex}100a = 100{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{100}{100}{/tex}
{tex}a = 1{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}a = 1{/tex} and {tex}b = 273.15{/tex}.

questions.question: Which of the following expressions is a polynomial?
questions.explanation:
We have: x2 + {tex}\frac{2 x^{3 / 2}}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} + 6 = x2 + 2x3/2 x-1/2 + 6
= x2 + 2x + 6
It is a polynomial because it has only non-negative integral powers of x

questions.question: A parking lot charges an initial fee of ₹30 and an hourly rate of ₹10. If the total cost for parking is ₹80, for how many hours was the vehicle parked?
questions.explanation:
Let the number of hours be {tex}h{/tex}.

Initial parking fee = ₹30
Hourly charge = ₹10
Therefore, total cost:

{tex} 30+10 h{/tex}

Given total cost {tex}=₹ 80{/tex}

{tex} 30+10 h=80{/tex}

Subtract 30 from both sides:

{tex} 10 h=50{/tex}

Divide both sides by 10 :

{tex} h=5{/tex}

Therefore, the vehicle was parked for {tex}{5}{/tex} hours.

questions.question: A service charges a reservation fee of ₹70 and an additional ₹12 per hour of service. If a customer paid a total of ₹190, for how many hours did they use the service?
questions.explanation:
Let 'h' be the number of hours.
The total cost can be represented as 70 + 12h.
The total cost is ₹190.
So, 70 + 12h = 190.
{tex}12 h=120{/tex}
{tex}h=10{/tex}

questions.question: The maximum number of terms in a polynomial of degree 10 is
questions.explanation:
The maximum number of terms is one more than the power of the polynomial.
Therefore, the maximum number of terms in a polynomial of degree 10 is (10 + 1) = 11

questions.question: A fruit vendor charges a base fee of ₹150 for delivery plus ₹25 for every kilogram of fruit ordered. If 'k' represents the number of kilograms of fruit, which of the following expressions represents the total cost?
questions.explanation:
The base fee of ₹150 is a fixed cost, and ₹25 is charged for *every* kilogram (k).
So, the cost dependent on kilograms is 25k, and the total cost is 25k + 150.

questions.question: Which of the following is a polynomial in one variable?
questions.explanation: Clearly, {tex}\sqrt{2} x^{2}-\sqrt{3} x{/tex} + 6 is a polynomial in one variable because it has only non-negative integral powers of x.

questions.question: Which of the following is a binomial?
questions.explanation: Clearly, x2 + 4 is an expression having two non-zero terms. So, it is a binomial.

questions.question: A company's revenue {tex}R(q){/tex} from selling {tex}q{/tex} units of a product is given by {tex}R(q) = 25q - C{/tex}, where {tex}C{/tex} is the initial fixed cost. If the initial fixed cost is ₹150 and the revenue is ₹500, how many units were sold?
questions.explanation:
Given the revenue function {tex}R(q) = 25q - C{/tex}.
The initial fixed cost {tex}C = 150{/tex}.
So, the function is {tex}R(q) = 25q - 150{/tex}.
We are given that the revenue is ₹500, so {tex}R(q) = 500{/tex}.
{tex}500 = 25q - 150{/tex}
Add 150 to both sides: {tex}500 + 150 = 25q{/tex}
{tex}650 = 25q{/tex}
Divide by 25: {tex}q = \frac{650}{25}{/tex}
{tex}q = 26{/tex}
Therefore, 26 units were sold.

questions.question: A library charges a fixed membership fee of ₹15 plus ₹2.50 per book borrowed. If a member spent a total of ₹50, how many books did they borrow?
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}B{/tex} be the number of books borrowed and {tex}C{/tex} be the total cost.
The cost function is {tex}C = 15 + 2.50B{/tex}.
Given total cost {tex}C = 50{/tex}.
{tex}50 = 15 + 2.50B{/tex}
Subtract 15 from both sides: {tex}50 - 15 = 2.50B{/tex}
{tex}35 = 2.50B{/tex}
Divide by 2.50:
{tex}B = \frac{35}{2.50}{/tex}
{tex}B = 14{/tex}
Therefore, the member borrowed 14 books.

questions.question: The monomial of degree 50 is
questions.explanation:
{tex}2{x^{50}}{/tex}

Monomial means only one term and degree means highest power.

questions.question: A sequence follows the rule: 3n + 2. Which term appears at the 7th stage?
questions.explanation:
Substitute {tex}n=7{/tex} :

{tex}3(7)+2{/tex}
{tex}21+2=23{/tex}

Therefore, the 7th term is 23.

questions.question: The cost of manufacturing a certain product includes a fixed overhead cost and a per-unit cost. If producing 50 units costs ₹1500 and producing 150 units costs ₹3500, what is the fixed overhead cost?
questions.explanation:
Let 'y' be the total cost and 'x' be the number of units.
The relationship is {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}, where 'b' is the fixed overhead cost and 'a' is the per-unit cost.
For 50 units: {tex}1500 = 50a + b{/tex} (Equation 1)
For 150 units: {tex}3500 = 150a + b{/tex} (Equation 2)
Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2: {tex}(3500 - 1500) = (150a - 50a) + (b - b){/tex}
{tex}2000 = 100a{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{2000}{100}{/tex}
{tex}a = 20{/tex}
The per-unit cost 'a' is ₹20.
Substitute {tex}a = 20{/tex} into Equation 1:
{tex}1500 = 50(20) + b{/tex}
{tex}1500 = 1000 + b{/tex}
{tex}b = 1500 - 1000{/tex}
{tex}b = 500{/tex}
The fixed overhead cost 'b' is ₹500.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a linear pattern where a quantity increases by a constant amount over equal intervals?
questions.explanation: Linear growth describes a linear pattern where a quantity increases by a constant amount over equal intervals.

questions.question: The degree of the zero polynomial is
questions.explanation:
The general form of a polynomial is anxn, where n is a natural number.
For zero polynomial an = 0.
Since the largest value of n for which an is non-zero is negative infinity (all the integers are bigger than negative infinity).
Therefore, the degree of zero polynomials is not defined.

questions.question: A rental car charges a base fee of ₹40 and an additional ₹0.75 per mile driven. If C is the total cost and m is the number of miles driven, which linear function represents the cost?
questions.explanation:
The base fee of ₹40 is a constant, and ₹0.75 is charged for each mile driven.
Therefore, the total cost {tex}C(m){/tex} is the base fee plus the per-mile charge multiplied by the number of miles, resulting in {tex}C(m) = 40 + 0.75m{/tex}.

questions.question: Degree of cubic polynomial with two terms is -
questions.explanation:
A cubic polynomial is a polynomial of degree three.
e,g, ax3 + b

questions.question: A sequence of numbers follows the pattern 4n - 1. Which stage will contain 75 units?
questions.explanation:
Let the stage be 'n'.
We are given that {tex}4n - 1 = 75{/tex}.
Add 1 to both sides: {tex}4n = 75 + 1 4n = 76{/tex}.
Divide by 4: {tex}n = 76 / 4 {/tex}.

n = 19
So, the 19th stage will contain 75 units.

questions.question: A service charges a reservation fee of ₹70 and an additional ₹12 per hour of service. If a customer paid a total of ₹190, for how many hours did they use the service?
questions.explanation:
Let 'h' be the number of hours.
The total cost can be represented as 70 + 12h.
The total cost is ₹190.
So, 70 + 12h = 190.
{tex}12 h=120{/tex}
{tex}h=10{/tex}

questions.question: The degree of the polynomial -x5 is:
questions.explanation: Highest exponent of the variable is 5.

questions.question: Degree of non zero constant polynomial is -
questions.explanation:
As power of the leading coefficient of the Constant polynomial is 0, hence the highest degree of this polynomial is zero.
e,g, 7 = 7 x0 is a constant polynomial.
So Its degree = 0.

questions.question: A fitness club offers two membership options: Plan A charges ₹25 as a joining fee and ₹15 per month. Plan B charges ₹10 as a joining fee and ₹20 per month. After how many months will the total cost of both plans be equal?
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}m{/tex} be the number of months.

Cost of Plan A:

{tex} C_A=25+15 m{/tex}

Cost of Plan B:

{tex} C_B=10+20 {~m}{/tex}

For the costs to be equal:

{tex} 25+15 m=10+20 m{/tex}

Subtract 10 from both sides:

{tex} 15+15 {~m}=20 {~m}{/tex}

Subtract 15 m from both sides:

{tex} 15=5 m{/tex}

Divide by 5:

{tex} m=3{/tex}

Therefore, the total cost of both plans will be equal after 3 months.

questions.question: In the linear relationship {tex}y = -2x + 7{/tex}, what do 'a' and 'b' represent, respectively?
questions.explanation:
In the standard form of a linear relationship {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}, 'a' is the coefficient of x and 'b' is the constant term.
In the given equation {tex}y = -2x + 7{/tex}, comparing it to the standard form: 'a' is -2 (the coefficient of x) 'b' is 7 (the constant term)

questions.question: For axp + bxq + c to be polynomial p & q are:
questions.explanation: This follows from the definition of polynomials.

questions.question: If a line has a slope of {tex}-5{/tex} and passes through the point {tex}(0, 8){/tex}, what is its equation?
questions.explanation: The slope {tex}a = -5{/tex}. Since the line passes through {tex}(0, 8){/tex}, the y-intercept {tex}b = 8{/tex}. Using the form {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}, the equation is {tex}y = -5x + 8{/tex}.

questions.question: In the context of the cost function {tex}C(d) = 100 + 60d{/tex}, what does the constant '60' represent?

options.html: The cost increases for every unit of distance traveled

questions.explanation:
In the function {tex}C(d)=100+60 d{/tex}, the number 60 tells us how much the cost increases for each kilometre travelled.
So, every extra kilometre adds ₹60 to the total cost.

questions.question: The coefficient of x2 in the polynomial 2x3 + 4x2 + 3x + 1
questions.explanation:
A coefficient is a multiplicative factor in a term of a polynomial or any expression.
Therefore, the coefficient of x2 in the polynomial 2x3 + 4x2 + 3x + 1 is 4.

questions.question: If deg (f(x)) = 5 & deg (g(x)) = 4 then deg [f(x) - g(x)] is.
questions.explanation:
5
If deg(f(x)) = m and deg(f(x)) = n then deg(f(x) {tex}\pm{/tex} g(x)) = min(m ,n)

questions.question: A polynomial containing two non-zero terms is called a ________.
questions.explanation:
A polynomial containing two non-zero terms is called a binomial.
Example: 3x + 4, 5y + 1, x2 + x

questions.question: A stock's value initially was ₹250. It decreases by ₹5.50 each day. Find the value of the stock after 15 days.
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}V(d){/tex} be the value of the stock after {tex}d{/tex} days. The initial value is ₹250. It decreases by ₹5.50 each day.
So, the linear function for the stock's value is {tex}V(d) = 250 - 5.50d{/tex}.
To find the value after 15 days, substitute {tex}d = 15{/tex}:
{tex}V(15) = 250 - 5.50(15){/tex}
{tex}V(15) = 250 - 82.50{/tex}
{tex}V(15) = 167.50{/tex}
The value of the stock after 15 days will be ₹167.50.

questions.question: A linear relationship between two variables x and y can be expressed in which of the following forms?
questions.explanation: A linear relationship represents the relationship between two variables x and y, and is generally expressed in the form {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}, where 'a' is the coefficient of x (slope) and 'b' is the constant term (y-intercept).

questions.question: If a relationship between two quantities x and y is given by {tex}y = 3x - 5{/tex}, what is the value of y when x = 4?
questions.explanation: To find the value of y when x = 4, substitute x = 4 into the given linear relationship: {tex}y = 3x - 5{/tex} {tex}y = 3(4) - 5{/tex} {tex}y = 12 - 5{/tex} {tex}y = 7{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following is/are polynomial(s):
questions.explanation: {tex}{/tex}The above expression is a polynomial as power of x is a whole number

questions.question: Which of the following is a linear polynomial?
questions.explanation: {tex}x+1{/tex} is a linear polynomial because its degree is 1.

questions.question: Lines with equal slopes but different y-intercepts are always:
questions.explanation: Lines with the same slope but different y-intercepts are parallel to each other. They have the same steepness but cross the y-axis at different points.

questions.question: The terms of the polynomial x3 - 4x2 - 3x + 2 are:
questions.explanation: Clearly,there are 4 terms as there are 4 signs , +,-,-,+

questions.question: The degree of constant function is
questions.explanation:
The degree of any constant term 5 (say)

We can write 5 as 5 x 1 = 5xo          [Since ao= 1]

Therefore the degree of any constant term is 0

questions.question: Which of the following is a quadratic polynomial?
questions.explanation: x2 + 5x + 4 is a polynomial of degree 2. So, it is a quadratic polynomial.

questions.question: Which of the following equations represents a linear relationship where the y-coordinate is three times the x-coordinate?
questions.explanation: A linear relationship where the y-coordinate is three times the x-coordinate is directly represented by the equation {tex}y = 3x{/tex}.

questions.question: Which type of slope indicates a linear growth?
questions.explanation: A linear growth is represented by a straight line with a positive slope.

questions.question: If a point {tex}(p, q){/tex} lies on the line defined by the equation {tex}y = 5x - 2{/tex}, what must be true about the relationship between {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex}?
questions.explanation: If a point {tex}(p, q){/tex} lies on the line {tex}y = 5x - 2{/tex}, its coordinates must satisfy the equation. Therefore, substituting {tex}x=p{/tex} and {tex}y=q{/tex} yields {tex}q = 5p - 2{/tex}.

questions.question: A rally starts with 200 participants. Each hour, 15 participants drop out. How many participants will remain after 8 hours?
questions.explanation:
The initial number of participants is 200.
Each hour, 15 participants drop out.
So, after 8 hours, the number of participants who dropped out is {tex}15 \times 8 = 120{/tex}.
The number of participants remaining is {tex}200 - 120 = 80{/tex}.

questions.question: A book has 600 pages. A student reads 30 pages every day. If 'd' represents the number of days, what is the linear expression for the number of pages left after 'd' days?
questions.explanation:
Initial number of pages = 600
Pages read each day = 30
After {tex}\boldsymbol{d}{/tex} days, pages read: {tex} 30 d{/tex}

Therefore, pages left:

{tex} 600-30 d{/tex}

questions.question: The coefficient of {tex}{x^3}{/tex} in {tex}2x + {x^2} - 5{x^3} + {x^4}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: The coefficient of {tex}{x^3}{/tex} in {tex}2x + {x^2} - 5{x^3} + {x^4}{/tex} is {tex}-5.{/tex}

questions.question: A polynomial of degree 3 in x has at most
questions.explanation: 4 terms of not more than the power of 3.

questions.question: A scientist is studying the growth of a certain plant. The height of the plant 'h' (in cm) after 'd' days follows a linear relationship {tex}h = ad + b{/tex}. On day 5, the plant was 18 cm tall. On day 12, it was 39 cm tall. What was the initial height of the plant (on day 0)?
questions.explanation:
Let 'h' be the height and 'd' be the number of days.
The linear relationship is {tex}h = ad + b{/tex}.
On day 5: When {tex}d = 5, h = 18{/tex}. So, {tex}18 = 5a + b{/tex} (Equation 1)
On day 12: When {tex}d = 12, h = 39{/tex}. So, {tex}39 = 12a + b{/tex} (Equation 2)
Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2: {tex}(39 - 18) = (12a - 5a) + (b - b){/tex}
{tex}21 = 7a{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{21}{7}{/tex}
{tex}a = 3{/tex}
The growth rate 'a' is 3 cm/day.
Substitute {tex}a = 3{/tex} into Equation 1: {tex}18 = 5(3) + b{/tex}
{tex}18 = 15 + b{/tex}
{tex}b = 18 - 15{/tex}
{tex}b = 3{/tex}
The initial height 'b' (on day 0, when d=0) was 3 cm.

questions.question:
Two delivery services charge according to the following rules:
Service A: ₹40 fixed charge + ₹12 per package

Service B: ₹70 fixed charge + ₹6 per package

For how many packages will both services charge the same amount?
questions.explanation:
Let the number of packages be {tex}p{/tex}.
Cost of Service A:

{tex} 40+12 p{/tex}

Cost of Service B:

{tex} 70+6 p{/tex}

Equating both costs:

{tex}40+12 p=70+6 p{/tex}
{tex}6 p=30{/tex}
{tex}p=5{/tex}

Therefore, both services charge the same amount for 5 packages.

questions.question: A store offers a discount on bulk purchases. The total price 'y' for 'x' items can be represented by a linear relationship {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}. If buying 10 items costs ₹320 and buying 25 items costs ₹770, what is the price per item ('a') and what is the fixed discount/charge ('b')?
questions.explanation:
Given the linear relationship {tex}y = ax + b{/tex}.
For 10 items: {tex}320 = 10a + b{/tex} (Equation 1)
For 25 items: {tex}770 = 25a + b{/tex} (Equation 2)
Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2: {tex}(770 - 320) = (25a - 10a) + (b - b){/tex}
{tex}450 = 15a{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac{450}{15}{/tex}
{tex}a = 30{/tex}
The price per item 'a' is ₹30.
Substitute {tex}a = 30{/tex} into Equation 1: {tex}320 = 10(30) + b{/tex}
{tex}320 = 300 + b{/tex}
{tex}b = 320 - 300{/tex}
{tex}b = 20{/tex}
The fixed discount/charge 'b' is ₹20.
(It means there's a fixed charge of ₹20, not a discount in this case, or it implies an initial cost before any items are counted as part of larger discount systems.
Since 'a' is positive, 'b' is a fixed charge.)

questions.question: The coefficient of x2 in (2 - 3x2)(x2 - 5) is
questions.explanation:
(2 - 3x2)(x2 - 5)
= 2x2 - 10 - 3x4 + 15x2 
= -3x4 + 17x2 - 10
Therefore, the coefficient of x2 is 17.

When we divide 1 by 999 its result is 0.001001001001001........

{tex} {0}{{.}}\overline{{001}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{999}{/tex}

questions.question: The difference of a rational and an irrational number is always
questions.explanation:
Rational Numbers say {tex}4 \over 9{/tex}, {tex}p \over q{/tex}, {tex}\sqrt4{/tex}, fraction, whole numbers, terminating decimal, repeating decimal, perfect square,  can be expressed as a ratio of two integers provided the denominator is not equal to zero
Irrational Numbers {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} ,{tex}\sqrt 5{/tex} ,{tex}\sqrt 7{/tex} , {tex}\pi{/tex} not a fraction, decimal does not repeat, decimal does not end, non-perfect square,  we cannot express as a ratio but both can be expressed as decimal numbers
The difference between a rational and an irrational number is always an irrational number.
e.g. rational - irrational = irrational   say 2 - {tex} \sqrt2{/tex}  = irrational

questions.question: {tex}\sqrt[4]{\sqrt[3]{3^2}}{/tex} can be expressed as:
questions.explanation:
We know that,
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt[n]{x}=x^{\frac{1}{n}} {/tex}
Solving,
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt[4]{\sqrt[3]{3^2}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt[4]{3^{\frac{2}{3}}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 3^{\frac{2}{3 \times 4}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 3 \frac{2}{12} {/tex}
{tex} =3^{\frac{1}{6}} {/tex}

questions.question: {tex}\frac{5-\sqrt{7}}{5+\sqrt{7}}-\frac{5+\sqrt{7}}{5-\sqrt{7}}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex} \text { Given, } {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{5-\sqrt{7}}{5+\sqrt{7}}-\frac{5+\sqrt{7}}{5-\sqrt{7}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{(5-\sqrt{7})^2-(5+\sqrt{7})^2}{(5+\sqrt{7})(5-\sqrt{7})} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex} \frac{(5)^2+(\sqrt{7})^2-2 \times 5 \times \sqrt{7}-\left[(5)^2+(\sqrt{7})^2+2] \right.}{5^2-(\sqrt{7})^2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{25+7-10 \sqrt{7}-[25+7+10 \sqrt{7}]}{25-7} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{25-25+7-7-10 \sqrt{7}-10 \sqrt{7}}{18} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{-20 \sqrt{7}}{18} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{-10 \sqrt{7}}{9} {/tex}

questions.question: Let zero {tex}=\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers. What additional condition will make {tex}0=\frac{p}{q}{/tex} a rational number:
questions.explanation: {tex}0=\frac{p}{q}{/tex} is rational as long as {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex} are integers and {tex}q \neq 0{/tex}.

questions.question: What is meant by a 'pure repeating decimal'?

options.html: A decimal in which a digit or a sequence of digits begins repeating immediately after the decimal point.
A decimal with a finite number of digits.
A decimal where the digits after the decimal point do not repeat.
A decimal where all digits are the same.

questions.explanation: A pure repeating decimal is characterized by the repeating block of digits starting immediately after the decimal point, with no non-repeating digits in between.

questions.question: {tex}1 . \overline{9}-1.9{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\mathrm{x}=1 . \overline{9}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=1.999 {/tex} ...(1)
{tex} \Rightarrow 10 x=19.999{/tex} ...(2)
Subtracting equation (1) from (2), we get :
{tex} \Rightarrow 10 {x}-{x}=19.999 \ldots-1.999 \ldots {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9 {x}=18 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {x}=\frac{18}{9}=2 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1 . \overline{9}-1.9 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2-1.9 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 0.1 {/tex}

questions.question: Between two rational numbers

options.html: there is exactly one rational number
there are infinitely many rational numbers
there are only rational numbers and no irrational number

questions.explanation:
Between two rational number, there infinitely many rational number.
e.g., {tex}\frac{3}{5} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} are two rational number, then {tex}\frac{31}{50}, \frac{32}{50}, \frac{33}{50}, \frac{34}{50}, \frac{35}{50}{/tex}, ..... are infinite rational numbers between them.

questions.question: A shepherd uses pebbles to count his flock. For each sheep that leaves the pen, he puts 1 pebble into a bag. For each sheep that returns, he removes 1 pebble. If 25 sheep left and 23 returned, how many pebbles remain in the bag, indicating how many sheep are still out?
questions.explanation:
Number of sheep that left = 25
Number of pebbles put into the bag = 25
Number of sheep that returned = 23
Number of pebbles removed from the bag = 23
Number of pebbles remaining in the bag = Number of pebbles put in - Number of pebbles removed
25 - 23 = 2
 Pebbles remaining = 2.
This indicates 2 sheep are still out.

questions.question: The number {tex}0.\overline{7}{/tex} in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q},{/tex} where p and q are integers and {tex}q \neq 0,{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{7}{9}{/tex}
Let x = 0.777... ----(i)
multiply both side by 10, we get
10 x = 7.777... -----(ii)
subtracting (i) from (ii), we get,
10 x- x = 7.777.... - 0.777...
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 x = 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{7}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: The sum of {tex}0 . \overline{3}{/tex} and {tex}0 . \overline{4}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}0 . \overline{3}{/tex} + {tex}0 . \overline{4}{/tex}
={tex}0 . \overline{7}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{7}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: Convert the general repeating decimal {tex}3.0\overline{1}{/tex} into a fraction in its simplest form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}x = 3.0\overline{1}{/tex}.
Step 1: Shift the decimal to place the non-repeating part ({tex}0{/tex}) just after the decimal point.
Multiply by {tex}10^1 = 10{/tex}:
{tex}10x = 30.\overline{1}{/tex} (Equation 1)
Step 2: Now, to move one full repeating cycle ({tex}1{/tex}) before the decimal point, multiply by {tex}10^1 = 10{/tex} (relative to {tex}10x{/tex}):
{tex}10 \times (10x) = 10 \times (30.\overline{1}){/tex}
{tex}100x = 301.\overline{1}{/tex} (Equation 2)
Step 3: Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2:
{tex}100x - 10x = 301.\overline{1} - 30.\overline{1}{/tex}
{tex}90x = 271{/tex}
Step 4: Solve for {tex}x{/tex}:
{tex}x = \frac{271}{90}{/tex}
Since 271 is a prime number, the fraction cannot be simplified further.
Therefore, {tex}3.0\overline{1} = \frac{271}{90}{/tex}.

questions.question: {tex}7.\overline 2 {/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}7 . \overline{2}{/tex} = 7 + {tex}0 . \overline{2}{/tex} 
i.e., 7 + {tex}\frac{2}{9}{/tex} 
i.e., {tex}\frac{63+2}{9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{65}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: What collective term is used for the set of all rational and irrational numbers?
questions.explanation: Real Numbers (R) are formed by combining both rational and irrational numbers, forming the entire real number line.

questions.question: If {tex}a=2+\sqrt{3}, {/tex}  then the value of {tex}\frac{1}{a}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}2-\sqrt{3}{/tex}
x = {tex}2+\sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If n is a natural number, then {tex}\sqrt{n}{/tex} is

options.html: sometimes a natural number and sometimes an irrational number

questions.explanation:
If n is a natural number, then {tex}\sqrt{n}{/tex} is

 then {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational

Which is rational
hence If n is a natural number, then {tex}\sqrt{n}{/tex} is sometimes a natural number and sometimes an irrational number

questions.question: A 'general repeating decimal' can be described as a decimal that has which of the following characteristics?

options.html: Digits that never repeat and never terminate.
Some non-repeating digits just after the decimal point, followed by a repeating block.
Only repeating digits after the decimal point.

questions.explanation: A general repeating decimal starts with some non-repeating digits after the decimal point, and then a block of digits begins to repeat infinitely.

questions.question: Convert the pure repeating decimal {tex}0.\overline{7}{/tex} into a fraction in its simplest form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}x = 0.\overline{7}{/tex}.
Since one digit is repeating, multiply both sides by {tex}10^1 = 10{/tex}
: {tex}10x = 7.\overline{7}{/tex}
Subtract the original equation from this new equation:
{tex}10x - x = 7.\overline{7} - 0.\overline{7}{/tex}
{tex}9x = 7{/tex}
{tex}x = \frac{7}{9}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}0.\overline{7} = \frac{7}{9}{/tex}.

questions.question: A student is charting the growth of a plant and records its height as 12 cm. She later notes that the ideal plant growth factor involves a constant similar to {tex}\sqrt{10}{/tex} per week. What is the fundamental difference in number classification between '12' and '{tex}\sqrt{10}{/tex}'?

options.html: 12 is an integer, {tex}\sqrt{10}{/tex} is an irrational number.

questions.explanation: 12 is a whole number and thus an integer. {tex}\sqrt{10}{/tex} cannot be expressed as a simple fraction and has a non-terminating, non-repeating decimal, making it an irrational number.

questions.question: If {tex}\mathrm{x}=5+2 \sqrt{6}{/tex}, then {tex}x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}={/tex}
questions.explanation:
Given,
{tex} {x}=(5+2 \sqrt{6}) {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{(5+2 \sqrt{6})} {/tex}
Rationalizing,
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{5+2 \sqrt{6}} \times \frac{(5-2 \sqrt{6})}{(5-2 \sqrt{6})} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{5-2 \sqrt{6}}{5^2-(2 \sqrt{6})^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{5-2 \sqrt{6}}{25-24} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{5-2 \sqrt{6}}{1} {/tex}
{tex} =5-2 \sqrt{6} . {/tex}
By formula,
{tex} x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^2-2 {/tex}
Substituting values we get:
{tex} \Rightarrow x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}=(5+2 \sqrt{6}+5-2 \sqrt{6})^2-2 {/tex}
{tex} =10^2-2 {/tex}
{tex} =100-2 {/tex}
{tex} =98 . {/tex}

questions.question: After rationalising the denominator of {tex}\frac{7}{3 \sqrt{3}-2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}, we get the denominator as
questions.explanation:
{tex} \frac{7}{3 \sqrt{3}-2 \sqrt{2}} {/tex}
Let us rationalise the denominator,
Then,
{tex} \frac{7}{3 \sqrt{3}-2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{7}{3 \sqrt{3}-2 \sqrt{2}} \times \frac{3 \sqrt{3}+2 \sqrt{2}}{3 \sqrt{3}+2 \sqrt{2}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{7(3 \sqrt{3}+2 \sqrt{2})}{(3 \sqrt{3})^2-(2 \sqrt{2})^2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{7 \times 3 \sqrt{3}+14 \times \sqrt{2}}{27-8} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{21 \sqrt{3}+14 \sqrt{2}}{19} {/tex}

questions.question: If ‘m’ is a positive integer which is not a perfect square, then {tex}\sqrt m {/tex} is
questions.explanation:
In √m ,if m is not perfect square ,then the value of √m will be m½.

example, √3 ,where 3 is not a perfect square then , √3 = 3½, which is irrational

But if it is perfect square then value of  √m = some integer

Example– √4 =2

questions.question: {tex}(-2-\sqrt{3})(-2+\sqrt{3}){/tex} when simplified is
questions.explanation:
{tex}(-2-\sqrt{3})(-2+\sqrt{3}){/tex}
= {tex}(-2)^2-(\sqrt{3})^2{/tex}
= 4-3
=1
positive and rational

questions.question: When constructing a line segment of length {tex}\sqrt{n}{/tex} on the number line using the method described, if OA = 1 unit and a perpendicular AB is drawn such that AB = 2 units, what will be the length of the diagonal OB?
questions.explanation:
Using the Pythagorean theorem: {tex}OB^2 = OA^2 + AB^2{/tex}.
Given {tex}OA = 1{/tex} and {tex}AB = 2{/tex}, then {tex}OB^2 = 1^2 + 2^2 = 1 + 4 = 5{/tex}.
Therefore, {tex}OB = \sqrt{5}{/tex} units.

questions.question: Convert the general repeating decimal {tex}3.0\overline{1}{/tex} into a fraction in its simplest form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}x = 3.0\overline{1}{/tex}.
Step 1: Shift the decimal to place the non-repeating part ({tex}0{/tex}) just after the decimal point.

{tex}90x = 271{/tex}

questions.question: Convert the terminating decimal 0.64 into a fraction in its simplest form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
The decimal 0.64 can be written as {tex}\frac{64}{100}{/tex}.
To simplify, divide both the numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor, which is 4. {tex}\frac{64 \div 4}{100 \div 4} = \frac{16}{25}{/tex}.
So, {tex}0.64 = \frac{16}{25}{/tex}.

questions.question: (2+ {tex}\sqrt5{/tex}) is
questions.explanation: The sum of a rational and an irrational number is an irrational number hence it is an irrational number.

questions.question: {tex}0.6+0 . \overline{7}+0.4 \overline{7}{/tex} is equal to s:
questions.explanation:
Convert repeating decimals to fraction,
Let, {tex}x=0 . \overline{7}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=0.7777 {/tex} ...(1)
{tex} \Rightarrow 10 x=7.777 {/tex} ...(2)
Subtracting equation (1) from (2), we get :
{tex} \Rightarrow 10 x-x=7.777 \ldots . .0 .777 \ldots . {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9 x=7 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{7}{9} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Let, } y=0.4 \overline{7} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 10 y=4.7777 {/tex} ...(3)
{tex} \Rightarrow 100 y=47.777 {/tex} ...(4)
Subtracting equation (3) from (4), we get :
{tex} \Rightarrow 100 {y}-10 {y}=47.777 \ldots . .-4.777 \ldots . {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 90 {y}=43 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {y}=\frac{43}{90} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 0.6+0 . \overline{7}+0.4 \overline{7}=0.6+\frac{7}{9}+\frac{43}{90} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{6}{10}+\frac{7}{9}+\frac{43}{90} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{54+70+43}{90} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{167}{90} . {/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following numbers has terminating decimal representation?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{3}{7}=0.428571429 {/tex}... non-terminating decimal representation.
{tex}\frac{3}{5}=0.6{/tex} is terminating decimal representation.
{tex}\frac{1}{3}=0.33333 \ldots{/tex} non-terminating decimal representation.
{tex}\frac{3}{11}=0.272727273 .{/tex}.. non-terminating decimal representation.

questions.question: Evaluate the expression: {tex}15 - 0 - (-8) + (-4) \times 2{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
{tex}15 - 0 = 15{/tex}.
{tex}15 - (-8) = 15 + 8 = 23{/tex}.
{tex}(-4) \times 2 = -8{/tex}. Step 4:
: {tex}23 + (-8) = 23 - 8 = 15{/tex}.
The final value is 15.

questions.question: If {tex}\mathrm{x}=\sqrt{2}-1{/tex}, then {tex}\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^2{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Given,
{tex} {x}=\sqrt{2}-1 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}-1} {/tex}
Rationalizing,
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}-1} \times \frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}+1} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{(\sqrt{2})^2-(1)^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{2-1} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{1} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{2}+1 . {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^2=[\sqrt{2}-1-(\sqrt{2}+1)]^2 {/tex}
{tex} =[\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{2}-1-1]^2 {/tex}
{tex} =[-2]^2 {/tex}
{tex} =4 . {/tex}

questions.question: A stock's price decreases by ₹4 each day for 5 consecutive days. On the 6th day, its value increases by ₹12. If the initial price was ₹150, what is the final price of the stock?
questions.explanation:
Daily decrease: -₹4.
For 5 days: {tex}5 \times (-4) = -₹20{/tex}.
Increase on 6th day: +₹12.
Initial price: ₹150.
Final price: {tex}150 - 20 + 12 = 130 + 12 = ₹142{/tex}.

questions.question: If you are trying to prove {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} is irrational using proof by contradiction, and you assume {tex}\sqrt{7} = p/q{/tex} where {tex}p, q{/tex} are co-prime integers, what will be the result after multiplying by {tex}q^2{/tex} and squaring both sides?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sqrt{7} = p/q{/tex}, squaring both sides gives {tex}7 = p^2/q^2{/tex}.
Multiplying by {tex}q^2{/tex} across the equation results in {tex}7q^2 = p^2{/tex}.

questions.question: A diver starts at the surface (0 meters). He descends 15 meters, then ascends 7 meters, and finally descends another 10 meters. What is his final depth relative to the surface?
questions.explanation:
Starting depth = 0 meters.
Descends 15 meters: {tex}0 - 15 = -15{/tex}.
Ascends 7 meters: {tex}-15 + 7 = -8{/tex}.
Descends 10 meters: {tex}-8 - 10 = -18{/tex}.
His final depth is -18 meters.

questions.question: The value of {tex}1.99 \overline{9}{/tex} in the form of {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}\mathrm{q} \neq 0{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
{tex} \text { Let } x=1.99 \overline{9} {/tex}
{tex} x=1.999999 {/tex} ...(1)

Multiplying both side with 10, we get
{tex} 10 x=19.99999{/tex} ...(2)
Subtracting equation (2) from equation (1), we get
{tex} \Rightarrow 10 x-x=19.99999 \ldots . .-1.99999 \ldots . . {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9 x=18 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{18}{9}=2 {/tex}

questions.question: Two rational numbers between {tex}-\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}-\frac{1}{7}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Let the first rational number between {tex}-\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}-\frac{1}{7}{/tex} be x.
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{3}{7}+\left(-\frac{1}{7}\right)\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{4}{7}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=-\frac{4}{14} {/tex}
Let the second rational number be y.
{tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{4}{14}+\left(-\frac{1}{7}\right)\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-4-2}{14}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{6}{14}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y=-\frac{6}{28}=-\frac{3}{14} {/tex}

questions.question: If {tex}\mathrm{x}=3+2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}, then {tex}x+\frac{1}{x}{/tex} equals to:
questions.explanation:
Given,
{tex} \Rightarrow {x}=3+2 \sqrt{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{(3+2 \sqrt{2})} {/tex}
Rationalizing,
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{(3+2 \sqrt{2})} \times \frac{(3-2 \sqrt{2})}{(3-2 \sqrt{2})} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{3-2 \sqrt{2}}{3^2-(2 \sqrt{2})^2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{3-2 \sqrt{2}}{9-8} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 3-2 \sqrt{2} {/tex}
Adding x and {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} we get
{tex} \Rightarrow x+\frac{1}{x}=3+2 \sqrt{2}+3-2 \sqrt{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 6 {/tex}

Which of the following is not a rational number?

{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}  = 1.4142135.......Hence, it is an irrational number.

questions.question: Convert the pure repeating decimal {tex}0.\overline{123}{/tex} into a fraction in its simplest form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}x = 0.\overline{123}{/tex}.
Since three digits are repeating, multiply both sides by {tex}10^3 = 1000{/tex}:
{tex}1000x = 123.\overline{123}{/tex}
Subtract the original equation from this new equation:
{tex}1000x - x = 123.\overline{123} - 0.\overline{123}{/tex}
{tex}999x = 123{/tex}
{tex}x = \frac{123}{999}{/tex}
Simplify the fraction by dividing numerator and denominator by 3:
{tex}x = \frac{123 \div 3}{999 \div 3} = \frac{41}{333}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}0.\overline{123} = \frac{41}{333}{/tex}.

questions.question: {tex}\frac{2+\sqrt{3}}{2-\sqrt{3}}-\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{2+\sqrt{3}}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\frac{2+\sqrt{3}}{2-\sqrt{3}}-\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{2+\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Solving,
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{(2+\sqrt{3}) \times(2+\sqrt{3})}{(2-\sqrt{3}) \times(2+\sqrt{3})}- {/tex}{tex} \frac{(2-\sqrt{3}) \times(2-\sqrt{3})}{(2+\sqrt{3}) \times(2-\sqrt{3})} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{(2+\sqrt{3})^2}{(2)^2-(\sqrt{3})^2}-\frac{(2-\sqrt{3})^2}{(2)^2-(\sqrt{3})^2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{(2)^2+(\sqrt{3})^2+2 \times 2 \times \sqrt{3}}{4-3}- {/tex}
{tex} \frac{(2)^2+(\sqrt{3})^2-2 \times 2 \times \sqrt{3}}{4-3} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{4+3+4 \sqrt{3}}{1}-\frac{4+3-4 \sqrt{3}}{1} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 7+4 \sqrt{3}-(7-4 \sqrt{3}) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 7+4 \sqrt{3}-7+4 \sqrt{3} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 8 \sqrt{3} {/tex}

questions.question: The number which is to be subtracted from {tex}\sqrt{72}{/tex} to get {tex}\sqrt{32}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex} \text { Let the number to be subtarcted be } {x} \text {. } {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt{72}-x=\sqrt{32} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{72}-\sqrt{32} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{36 \times 2}-\sqrt{16 \times 2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=6 \sqrt{2}-4 \sqrt{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=2 \sqrt{2} . {/tex}

questions.question: The product of any two irrational numbers is
questions.explanation:
The product of any two irrational numbers is sometimes rational, sometimes irrational
For example, {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} and {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} are two irrational number.
Their product {tex}2 \sqrt{3} \times 3 \sqrt{3}=6 \times(\sqrt{3})^2=6 \times 3={/tex} 18 which is rational number.
Again, let {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} and {tex}3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} be two irrational numbers.
Their product {tex}2 \sqrt{2} \times 3 \sqrt{3}=6 \times \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{3}=6 \sqrt{6}{/tex} which is an irrational number.

{tex} \Rightarrow 100 x-x=25.252525 \ldots . .-0.252525 \ldots \ldots {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 99 x=25 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{25}{99} {/tex}

questions.question: If {tex}\mathrm{x}=2-\sqrt{2}{/tex}, then {tex}x-\frac{1}{x}={/tex}
questions.explanation:
Given,
{tex} \Rightarrow {x}=2-\sqrt{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{(2-\sqrt{2})} {/tex}
Rationalizing the denominator, we get:
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{(2-\sqrt{2})} \times \frac{(2+\sqrt{2})}{(2+\sqrt{2})} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{2+\sqrt{2}}{2^2-(\sqrt{2})^2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{2+\sqrt{2}}{4-2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{2+\sqrt{2}}{2} {/tex}
Substituting values in {tex}x-\frac{1}{x}{/tex}, we get:
{tex} \Rightarrow 2-\sqrt{2}-\frac{2+\sqrt{2}}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{2(2-\sqrt{2})-(2+\sqrt{2})}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{4-2 \sqrt{2}-2-\sqrt{2}}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{2-3 \sqrt{2}}{2} {/tex}
{tex} x-\frac{1}{x}=\frac{2-3 \sqrt{2}}{2} {/tex}

questions.question: Only by inspecting the prime factors of the denominator, state which of the following fractions will be a terminating decimal?

A rational number will have a terminating decimal expansion if and only if the prime factorization of its denominator contains only powers of 2 and/or 5.
The prime factor of 16 is only 2.
{tex}\therefore \frac{3}{16}{/tex} will have a terminating decimal.

{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{4}{7}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=-\frac{4}{14} {/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{4}{14}+\left(-\frac{1}{7}\right)\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-4-2}{14}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{6}{14}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y=-\frac{6}{28}=-\frac{3}{14} {/tex}

questions.question: If {tex}\mathrm{x}=\sqrt{5}-2, x+\frac{1}{x}{/tex} is equal to:

{tex} x=\sqrt{5}-2 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}-2} {/tex}
Rationalizing,
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}-2} \times \frac{\sqrt{5}+2}{\sqrt{5}+2} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\sqrt{5}+2}{(\sqrt{5})^2-(2)^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\sqrt{5}+2}{5-4} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\sqrt{5}+2}{1} {/tex}
{tex} =\sqrt{5}+2 . {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x+\frac{1}{x}=\sqrt{5}-2+\sqrt{5}+2 {/tex}
{tex} =2 \sqrt{5} . {/tex}
